item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was ambivalent on it until the very last line of the interview, which pushed me over the line into a +1. Not only did it make me smile, but it's just a shade into the wonderfully surreal to establish the existence of a clown college with an accredited medical school.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last part made me laugh my ass off. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nthing the notion that the interview doesn't work for me. The description is great and does a lot of showing without telling, but then the interview tells everything, and that's no fun. There's also a lot of emphasis put on Fuller's violence, but the victim isn't given any personality or autonomy, so it ended up feeling hollow to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it a lot but I'm seriously confused on the timeline here.
So in 1880 he was transformed, but they discovered him panhandling in a city? How would no one have noticed a freakish, realistic elephant man? And what's the time spacing between him being "let go" and him being discovered? Surely there would be some hysteria about it.

+1, but maybe I am missing something

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ive been having an awful day today but this scp helped thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The central image is striking, particularly the artificial ivory valves, but the interview doesn't work for me at all. It's an overlong exposition dump (told by a guy who sole motivation seems to be spinning a yarn) for that robs the Circus of any sense of mystery or intrigue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interview as means of pure exposition is the new note-at-the-end; it's a tired means of easily conveying backstory, and doesn't give any impression beyond laziness.

It's excusable when the character is entertaining in their own right - your triplets being a pime example, fun in their audaciousness - but I've little to glean from this beyond a thin veneer of lore.

It doesn't make for a good SCP, let alone a good Circus character.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another reminder that Fuller didn't get half the kicking around he deserved. A little thin, but satisfying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My issues with this reside mostly in the dialogue. This is the image we're presented at the outset:

elderly, obese human male of African ancestry, exhibiting numerous elephantine traits. Most prominently, its nose and upper lip have been fused together and extended to a length of 30 cm, forming a semi-prehensile proboscis. This proboscis has had four ivory valves surgically installed on its upper portion, allowing SCP-4409 to produce music from it in a matter similar to a brass instrument.

Which translates into this

There are days I wish I'd never heard of it either. Fuller hired me as a labourer when I was still young and the Circus even younger. Despite all the weird exhibits Fuller had, life was pretty normal for me. That all changed when he heard of Joseph Merrick, the Elephant Man. He takes one good look at the photo in the newspaper and says 'Why, he barely looks like an elephant at all! I can do better than that!'.

It's not egregious, but it contrasts. I'm expecting a guy who can barely manage his sentences, wheeezing, on life support, psychologically scarred by a man without scruples about or distaste for fucking about with random humans' biology. Now, perhaps it wouldn't necessarily be that grimdark - as this seems like a piece underlined by some black comedy more than anything - but even for that purpose, I don't really buy it.

It's a great image, though.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: Agreed, you can’t leave a living sapient thing in the room and expect it not to die. You have to feed it, take care of it, etc.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: Technically, humanoids get Euclid classifications simply because they can't be locked up and forgot about; they have to be fed.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Another Herman Fuller SCP, this one going back to the early days of Herman's cruelty.

Before anyone says it, I know humanoids are normally Euclid, but this guy has severely limited mobility. He's not breaching containment on his own.

Thanks to everyone who read over this in chat.
